title: The Millennium

jokes.text: Saleswoman ignores customer trying to buy shoes while chatting on the phone about margaritas
jokes.setup: Elaine enters a store to buy huaraches and tries to get the saleswoman's attention
jokes.punchline: The saleswoman continues her personal phone call, completely ignoring the customer in front of her
jokes.explanation: A relatable observational premise — bad retail customer service — plays as setup for the episode's Putumayo rivalry plot. Functional but not especially inventive; the joke is mostly context-setting.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Excuse me, I'd like to buy something.

dialogue.speaker: Saleswoman
dialogue.line: Hold on, I'm talking to my friend.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I understand, but I'm a customer.

dialogue.speaker: Saleswoman
dialogue.line: Yeah, but you're not a friend.

jokes.text: 'Nothing. You just lost a customer.'
jokes.setup: After being ignored for over a minute, the saleswoman finally asks 'What can I do for you?'
jokes.punchline: Elaine delivers the perfectly timed exit line: 'Nothing. You just lost a customer.'
jokes.explanation: The timing and economy are excellent — the setup has been building for a full minute of screen time, and the punchline is delivered with maximum efficiency. The irony of 'Nothing' as the answer to 'What can I do for you?' is clean and satisfying.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You can start by acknowledging my existence.

jokes.text: 'I enjoy being the theater cutup. Last week after a preview, I yelled out, "Must miss!"'
jokes.setup: Jerry and his date are heading to the movies; he mentions he loves the previews
jokes.punchline: He reveals his idea of being a 'cutup' is shouting 'Must miss!' after a preview — a riff on the 'Must see' review blurb
jokes.explanation: Sharp wordplay on the critical blurb 'must-see,' flipped to 'must miss.' Perfectly characterizes Jerry's self-satisfied, slightly obnoxious wit. The word 'cutup' is itself an amusing self-description. Economical and original — a clever subversion that lands with confidence.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm a big fan of previews. I love going to see previews.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Must see TV? I'm all about 'must avoid TV.'

jokes.text: Jerry's date says she was in that theater and found the 'Must miss!' joke really funny
jokes.setup: Jerry smugly recounts his theater prank
jokes.punchline: His date validates him completely — a slightly absurd coincidence that also flatters Jerry's vanity perfectly
jokes.explanation: A minor beat that extends joke #3. The coincidence is mildly funny, and it sets up the speed-dial running gag by showing this is a new relationship. Works structurally but not independently funny.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I told her a joke at dinner and she loved it. She was laughing so hard she had to leave the table.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: George, that's wonderful. You should really consider doing this professionally.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You think so?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh absolutely. I'm sure the Comedy Store would love to have you.

jokes.text: Jerry is shocked that being on someone's speed dial at number seven after two dates requires lifting the plastic cover with a pen
jokes.setup: George reveals he's already number seven on his date's speed dial after only two dates
jokes.punchline: Jerry's response fixates entirely on the mechanical inconvenience of changing speed dial numbers — 'You've got to lift up that plastic thing with a pen' — completely sidestepping the emotional significance
jokes.explanation: Classic Seinfeld observational comedy — the detail about needing a pen to lift the plastic cover is both hyper-specific and completely accurate for the era. Jerry's redirect from romance to logistics is a perfect character beat. Establishes the speed dial as a rich comic concept for the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what the problem is with speed dial? It's like a commitment. Once you put someone on speed dial, you can't just take them off. You have to actually break up with them first.

jokes.text: Kramer is storing folding chairs in Jerry's apartment for a New Year's Eve party — in what turns out to be 1999's millennium celebration, over two and a half years away
jokes.setup: Kramer asks if he can keep some chairs at Jerry's for a New Year's Eve party
jokes.punchline: The party is the millennium celebration — 'New Year's Eve, 1999' — meaning he wants to store them for two and a half years
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Kramer — his grand scheme is absurd but delivered with total sincerity. The escalating reveal (New Year's → 1999 → two and a half years) is well-structured. It plants the episode's central absurdist premise with great efficiency.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You'll keep these chairs for eight months?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Eight months? Jerry, these chairs aren't going anywhere for two and a half years.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Two and a half years?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm gonna need them.

jokes.text: 'I got a case of party poppers I'm gonna keep in front of them.'
jokes.setup: Jerry points out Kramer is going to leave the chairs for two and a half years
jokes.punchline: Kramer's solution is to hide them with party poppers — stacking one absurd long-term plan on top of another
jokes.explanation: A nice escalation on the millennium chair bit. Kramer's solution — hiding chairs with party poppers — is earnest and therefore funny. Slight diminishing returns from joke #6 but still solid character work.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I got a storage unit. I'm keeping all my chairs there. I got about forty, fifty chairs in there.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Forty or fifty chairs? What are you doing with forty or fifty chairs?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Well, you never know when you're gonna need a chair, Jerry. Besides, I'm also storing a bunch of old calendars. You know, in case someone wants to know what day February 14th fell on in 1987.

jokes.text: 'I'm on a winning ball club, Jerry. They probably want to pick my brain.'
jokes.setup: George gets a call from the Mets front office wanting to take him to lunch
jokes.punchline: George's self-aggrandizing theory — that they want to 'pick his brain' — is absurd given that he's a low-level Yankees employee whose 'brain' has produced nothing of value
jokes.explanation: Classic George self-delusion — completely in character. The gap between George's self-image and reality is the engine of his comedy. Well-integrated into character without needing much setup.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: They want to have lunch with me. The Mets. They think I know something about baseball.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I mean, I don't know anything. But they don't know that.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So I'm gonna go in there, I'm gonna tell them exactly what I think. And they're gonna listen to me like I'm Branch Rickey or something.

jokes.text: 'Really, why do you think they're taking you out to lunch? / I have no idea.'
jokes.setup: George says the Mets probably want to pick his brain; Jerry asks 'Really, why do you think they're taking you out to lunch?'
jokes.punchline: George immediately pivots to 'I have no idea' — completely abandoning his previous theory with no acknowledgment of the contradiction
jokes.explanation: A tight, well-timed callback that deflates George's pomposity immediately. Jerry's line functions as a trap, and George walks right into it without realizing it. The economy here is exceptional — two lines, perfect beat.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Those Mayans at the store are so rude!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: The Mayans? What, like from ancient Mexico?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: No, Jerry, the people working there. Their names are Mayan or something.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh. I thought you meant like, the civilization that disappeared.

jokes.text: 'All right, I have had it with those Mayans.'
jokes.setup: The conversation at Jerry's shifts to Elaine's experience at Putumayo
jokes.punchline: Elaine opens with 'I have had it with those Mayans' — an absurdly broad ethnic complaint about a retail slight, delivered with complete conviction
jokes.explanation: A wonderfully absurd opening line. Blaming 'the Mayans' for a bad shopping experience is a perfect escalation of indignation — Elaine's grievance has inflated from one rude clerk to an entire ancient civilization. The line is extremely quotable and well-delivered as a cold entry into the scene.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You know, you should celebrate Cinco de Mayo. My friend Marcelino, he's got like a sixteenth of Mayan heritage.

jokes.text: Kramer's huarache expertise: 'No, no, not Putumayo. No, no, Cinco de Mayo. Yeah, Marcelino, he turned me onto it. And he's 1/64 Mayan.'
jokes.setup: Elaine complains about Putumayo; Kramer enthusiastically names a competitor and vouches for it via a fractional-Mayan connection
jokes.punchline: The specificity of '1/64 Mayan' as a credential for huarache knowledge is perfectly absurd — Kramer's chain of authority is both irrelevant and delivered with total confidence
jokes.explanation: The fraction '1/64' is the key comic detail — it's so vanishingly small as to be meaningless, yet Kramer cites it as serious provenance. Perfectly in character: Kramer's networks and connections are always absurdly hyper-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: These balloons won't stay inflated until New Year's.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I have balloons.

jokes.text: 'Kramer, these balloons aren't gonna stay filled until New Year's.' / 'Those aren't for New Year's. Those are my everyday balloons.'
jokes.setup: Jerry notes the balloons won't last until millennium New Year's
jokes.punchline: Kramer casually reveals he has 'everyday balloons' — a concept so mundane-yet-absurd that it recontextualizes Kramer's entire lifestyle
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. 'Those are my everyday balloons' is a perfect Kramer line — the concept of having balloons on a non-celebratory everyday basis is absurd, yet Kramer's casual delivery suggests it's completely normal to him. Maximum economy (five words), total character specificity, and deeply quotable. Rivals the top-tier Seinfeld joke anchors in its effortless absurdism.

dialogue.speaker: George Costanza
dialogue.line: Are you talking about getting me fired?

dialogue.speaker: Mets Executive 1
dialogue.line: No, no, no...

dialogue.speaker: Mets Executive 2
dialogue.line: Well, yes.

jokes.text: The Mets executives speak entirely in corporate doublespeak, telling George they need him fired without ever saying it: 'We're not talking. We're just talking.'
jokes.setup: The Mets want to hire George but can't while he's under Yankees contract; they need him fired
jokes.punchline: 'We're not talking. We're just talking.' — a perfectly circular non-statement that communicates everything while technically saying nothing
jokes.explanation: The corporate-speak parody is elegantly constructed. The exchange builds through several layers of deniability until 'We're not talking. We're just talking' — a paradox that is both funny and a perfect satire of plausible deniability. Strong structure and economy.

dialogue.speaker: George Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: We prefer to think of it as a mutual parting of ways.

dialogue.speaker: Mets Executive
dialogue.line: There was a realignment of personnel expectations.

dialogue.speaker: George Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: His skill set was no longer compatible with our organizational trajectory.

dialogue.speaker: Mets Executive
dialogue.line: We're exploring alternative career pathways for all stakeholders.

jokes.text: 'So you need me to get fired?' / 'We didn't say that.'
jokes.setup: George correctly decodes the executives' doublespeak
jokes.punchline: They deny it in the exact same language as before — another layer of the running corporate-deniability joke
jokes.explanation: Extends the doublespeak joke effectively. The callback to the same language pattern is consistent and the escalation works, though it's slightly diminished from the peak of #13.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I have to take this call. My machine is full.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Actually, you know what? You're not that interesting anyway.

jokes.text: 'But you are still paying for this lunch?' / 'We didn't say that.'
jokes.setup: The elaborate corporate deniability game has been established
jokes.punchline: George's concern cuts through all the intrigue to ask about the most pragmatic thing — who's paying — and gets the same stonewalling response
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character button on the deniability scene. The entire scene is about a major career move and George's one practical concern is whether they're covering the check. The 'We didn't say that' response applying the corporate deniability language to a lunch bill is a perfect comedic collision. Highly George-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I whipped Putumayo!

jokes.text: 'Sorry I'm late. There's a lot of chairs and balloons in my apartment.'
jokes.setup: Kramer arrives late to meet Jerry's date
jokes.punchline: His excuse directly references the chairs and balloons gag from earlier — the millennium party prep is already affecting his daily life
jokes.explanation: A tight callback that rewards attentive viewers. The chairs and balloons have migrated to Kramer's own apartment — the absurdist accumulation is growing. Good economy and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm gonna go in there and I'm gonna tell him exactly what I think. I don't care anymore.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Giddy up!

jokes.text: 'Yeah, I'm sorry about that Mongolian barbecue last night. I had heard good things. I don't know. It got a two in Zagat's.'
jokes.setup: Jerry offers to make up for a previous bad date by taking her somewhere nicer
jokes.punchline: He apologizes for the Mongolian barbecue — which got a two in Zagat's — revealing his idea of 'making it up to her' was already a step above a near-zero-rated restaurant
jokes.explanation: A solid observational bit about dating cheapness dressed as ignorance. 'It got a two in Zagat's' is the key — it implies Jerry knew (or could have known) it was terrible. Competent character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I want to go out in a blaze of glory.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, you'll certainly go out in a blaze of incompetence.

jokes.text: 'Let me just check my messages. Maybe a nicer girl called.'
jokes.setup: Jerry is on a date with a girl he apparently finds adequate but not exciting
jokes.punchline: He casually announces he's checking his messages in case a better woman has called — within earshot of his current date
jokes.explanation: A perfectly callous Jerry line delivered with complete unselfconsciousness. 'Maybe a nicer girl called' is supremely quotable — the economy is exceptional, and it perfectly captures Jerry's inability to commit combined with his total social obliviousness. The joke is also structurally smart: it seeds the speed-dial confusion that follows immediately.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You know, in the year 2000, we're gonna be eating a lot different. We're gonna be eating food that's made from solar power. They're gonna have these little solar panels on the food, they'll heat it up.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: And napkins... napkins are gonna be obsolete. We're gonna have these little robot arms that come out of the table and wipe our mouths for us.

jokes.text: Jerry checks his messages and Jane — his date's competitor — accidentally calls his number seven speed dial, which is now Jerry's number
jokes.setup: The speed dial system has been established as a complex social hierarchy; Jerry checks his messages
jokes.punchline: A woman named Jane calls number seven — which used to be Jerry but is now reassigned — creating immediate confusion about who is who
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed structural payoff — the speed-dial mechanics set up earlier now create real comic confusion. The misdirection is clean. This is good sitcom plotting comedy where the audience sees the collision coming a beat before the characters do.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's a scale. You know, it tells you how much things weigh.

jokes.text: 'Cinco de Mayo. Sales commission, bye-bye-o.'
jokes.setup: Kramer struts out of Cinco de Mayo, having made a large purchase to spite Putumayo
jokes.punchline: He rhymes 'Mayo' with 'bye-bye-o' in a self-congratulatory little song, completely pleased with himself
jokes.explanation: A fun Kramer moment — his little chant is perfectly in character, self-satisfied and slightly ridiculous. The rhyme is weak by design, which makes it funnier. Good character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You know, in the future, people won't need to talk anymore. They'll just communicate telepathically.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, you just think about what you wanna say, and the other person hears it.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: No more phones, no more letters... just pure thought transmission.

jokes.text: 'Good meeting?' / 'There was no meeting.' / 'But it was quite a meeting.'
jokes.setup: George returns from the Mets lunch
jokes.punchline: George corrects 'meeting' to 'there was no meeting' but immediately undercuts himself with 'But it was quite a meeting' — perfectly mimicking the executives' doublespeak from earlier
jokes.explanation: George has absorbed and now reproduces the executives' linguistic style — 'there was no meeting / but it was quite a meeting' — in three words showing his personality has been captured by their conspiratorial game. Tight, efficient, and a great callback.

dialogue.speaker: Valerie
dialogue.line: You're so thoughtful, Jerry. You got me flowers, you took me to that fancy restaurant, you surprised me with that weekend trip...

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, I like to show you how much you mean to me.

dialogue.speaker: Valerie
dialogue.line: I know, and now you're buying me jewelry? Jerry, this is too much!

jokes.text: 'The only thing is I have to get fired from the Yankees first.'
jokes.setup: George announces he's about to become the Mets' director of scouting
jokes.punchline: The casual 'only thing is' framing makes getting fired from his current job sound like a trivial logistical detail
jokes.explanation: The understatement works because of how nonchalantly George treats a major obstacle. Functional but doesn't hit as hard as the earlier George/Mets jokes.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm number one on her speed dial? That's great! I feel like General Patton.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I have successfully invaded her heart.

jokes.text: 'I want to walk away from the Yankees with people saying: "Wow! Now, that guy got canned."'
jokes.setup: George wants to get fired but wants to do it with distinction
jokes.punchline: His stated ambition is to be memorably, spectacularly fired — a perverse form of legacy-building
jokes.explanation: Quintessential George: his ambition is inverted — he wants excellence in failure. The phrase 'final blaze of incompetence' (from Jerry) and George's earnest nod is a great character beat. The concept of aspiring to be memorably fired is very funny and very George.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, I tried on Babe Ruth's uniform. It still fits pretty good.

jokes.text: 'So you want to go out in a final blaze of incompetence.'
jokes.setup: George expresses his desire to be spectacularly fired
jokes.punchline: Jerry perfectly summarizes George's plan with 'final blaze of incompetence' — a deadpan inversion of 'blaze of glory'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'Final blaze of incompetence' is a brilliantly economical inversion of 'blaze of glory' — it's the kind of neologism that feels instantly right and completely Seinfeld. The wordplay is effortless and highly quotable. This is exceptional economy: four words that perfectly capture an entire character arc and get a big laugh.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: These strawberries are pretty good. A little tart, but overall, I'd say... seven out of ten.

jokes.text: 'Remember at Dairy Queen where I cooled my feet in the soft-serve machine?'
jokes.setup: George and Jerry discuss his legendary history of getting fired
jokes.punchline: George casually references a past incident of extraordinary workplace misconduct — cooling his feet in a Dairy Queen soft-serve machine — as if it's a fond memory
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly specific invented backstory. The image of George putting his feet in a soft-serve machine at Dairy Queen is both disgusting and hysterical — and the casual way he invokes it as a cherished memory ('Remember when...') makes it even better. High originality, highly specific to George, and the mental image is irresistible. Ranks among the episode's top moments.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I'm throwing a millennium party.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: But I gotta make sure we got enough oxygen for everybody. I'm thinking about getting some scuba tanks, you know, just in case we run out of air.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Kramer, why would you run out of air at a party?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Well, with all those people, Jerry. And if we get really packed in there... we could be underwater.

jokes.text: 'Do you think people will still be using napkins in the year 2000? Or is this mouth-vacuum thing for real?'
jokes.setup: Kramer has been thinking about what the millennium will change
jokes.punchline: His millennium speculation has devolved to whether napkins will be replaced by a 'mouth-vacuum' — a concept as absurd as it is specific
jokes.explanation: Kramer's futurism is pitch-perfect: he's not speculating about technology or medicine, he's speculating about napkins vs. a mouth-vacuum. The specificity of 'mouth-vacuum thing' makes it wonderful — it implies this is something Kramer has actually heard about and is taking seriously. Top-tier Kramer absurdism.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh, that's what's causing the weather. Elaine's out there doing her little dance, and the sky's like, 'Alright, alright, I'll give her some rain.'

jokes.text: George has dropped two spots on the speed dial from 7 to 9 after a 'so-so date' and treats it as a relationship crisis
jokes.setup: George calls his date with Valerie 'so-so'
jokes.punchline: He's moved from number 7 to number 9 on the speed dial and treats this as a catastrophic relationship indicator
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the speed-dial concept. George's anxiety about dropping two spots mirrors his general insecurity in all relationships. The concept is extended well here.

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: I'm calling it the Newmillennium!

jokes.text: 'What is a barometer exactly?' / 'It's pronounced "thermometer."'
jokes.setup: George uses the word 'barometer' in its correct metaphorical sense ('the speed dial is like a relationship barometer')
jokes.punchline: Kramer corrects his usage of 'barometer' by saying 'It's pronounced thermometer' — confusing the two instruments entirely, delivered with total confidence
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's absolute best jokes. Kramer confidently 'corrects' George's correct use of 'barometer' by saying it's pronounced 'thermometer' — completely mixing up two different instruments while performing condescension. The joke has perfect structure (George uses the word correctly, Kramer corrects him wrongly), perfect character integration (only Kramer would do this with full confidence), and is extremely quotable. This is an 8+ joke by any measure.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Hamilton
dialogue.line: Jerry, I need to talk to you about something very important. I've been looking at your speed dial and I have to say, I'm very disappointed.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What? What's wrong with my speed dial?

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Hamilton
dialogue.line: What's wrong? I'm number seven, Jerry. Number seven! Do you know how that makes me feel? I'm your mother's best friend and I'm sandwiched between a pizza place and your dry cleaner!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, you know, the speed dial is really just for convenience...

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Hamilton
dialogue.line: Convenience? I've reorganized my entire life around speed dial positioning. I moved my hairdresser to Tuesdays so I'd be available when you might call. I've taken a course in speed dial psychology. I know that you check your speed dial every single day, and every single day I'm sitting there at number seven like some kind of second-rate acquaintance!

jokes.text: 'In the year 2000, we'll all be on speed dial. You just think of a person, they'll be talking to you.'
jokes.setup: George's speed-dial anxiety prompts Kramer to speculate about the future
jokes.punchline: Kramer's millennial tech prediction is that telepathic speed-dialing will exist — pure, earnest futurism with no basis in reality
jokes.explanation: Another Kramer millennium prediction — slightly less sharp than the mouth-vacuum line but still good character comedy. The telepathic dialing concept is funny because Kramer describes it with total sincerity.

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: You know what your problem is? You don't know the history of this game. Babe Ruth was the Sultan of Swat. The Sultan of Swat! You know what that means? He was a sultan. An actual sultan. From Swat. I looked it up.

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: Swat is a region in Pakistan. Ruth spent his winters there, perfecting his swing with the locals. That's why he was so good. The sultan training.

jokes.text: 'Oh, my God, number one! Seinfeld, you magnificent bastard.'
jokes.setup: Jerry checks Jane's speed dial after all his lavish gestures
jokes.punchline: He's reached number one — and his self-congratulation ('you magnificent bastard') reveals the entire romantic campaign was self-serving
jokes.explanation: Highly quotable. 'Seinfeld, you magnificent bastard' is a great character line — Jerry referring to himself in the third person while congratulating himself for gaming a woman's speed dial is perfectly encapsulated narcissism. Big laugh moment, strong payoff to the speed-dial plot.

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: I'm not putting on these pants. Do you know what Lou Gehrig died of?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: ALS.

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: Exactly. Now I'm supposed to put on his pants? What if it's in the fabric?

jokes.text: Kramer arrives wearing Babe Ruth's uniform from the Yankee Hall of Pride display case
jokes.setup: Kramer says he found something in the display case
jokes.punchline: He's wearing Babe Ruth's actual uniform, which he apparently just took
jokes.explanation: A great visual gag — Kramer casually wearing Babe Ruth's uniform as if he just borrowed a jacket. The nonchalance is the joke. Well-integrated with George's 'get fired' storyline since this is one of George's sabotage tools.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: How did you know to call me?

dialogue.speaker: Valerie
dialogue.line: My stepmother must have put you on my speed dial.

jokes.text: 'Isn't that Babe's Ruth uniform?' / 'Is it? Strawberries, anyone?'
jokes.setup: Jerry notices Kramer is wearing something significant
jokes.punchline: Kramer deflects with 'Is it? Strawberries, anyone?' — a perfectly casual non-answer that changes the subject via snack offer
jokes.explanation: A superb line. 'Is it? Strawberries, anyone?' is Kramer at his most casually criminal. The 'Is it?' feigns ignorance perfectly, and pivoting to offering strawberries is an absurdist dodge that reveals both guilt and complete confidence. Exceptional economy and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: Why would I cancel my party? You're just a bald fraud!

jokes.text: 'It's good. Juicy this time of year.' / 'Gotta get the good ones.'
jokes.setup: Kramer has just deflected suspicion about Babe Ruth's uniform by offering strawberries
jokes.punchline: Jerry and Kramer have a perfectly mundane exchange about strawberry quality while standing there with a stolen historical artifact — the domestic normalcy is the joke
jokes.explanation: The extension of the strawberry non-sequitur into a genuine domestic conversation about produce quality while apparently discussing stolen property is brilliantly straight-faced. The contrast between the gravity of the situation and the casualness of 'gotta get the good ones' is a great Seinfeld beat.

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: I've been planning this party for 21 years!

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: I'm going to have a DJ spinning vinyl records from the 80s.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Newman, it's 2005.

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: Exactly! The 80s will be back in style by then!

jokes.text: Elaine has been buying massive amounts of junk at Cinco de Mayo to dance in front of Putumayo and it's been causing rain
jokes.setup: Elaine explains her two-day campaign to hurt Putumayo by conspicuously shopping at their competitor
jokes.punchline: Jerry attributes the recent rain to Elaine's dancing and strutting outside Putumayo's storefront — treating her movement as a meteorological event
jokes.explanation: Jerry's throwaway 'No wonder we're getting so much rain' is a great deadpan reaction to Elaine's absurd campaign. The image of Elaine strutting outside a store for two days is inherently funny, and Jerry's rain comment lands well as a visual-comedy callback.

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: I will do it, but only on one condition: for the next thousand years, people will look up at the sky and see my name written in the stars.

jokes.text: 'Oh, no wonder we're getting so much rain.'
jokes.setup: Elaine describes her two days of dancing outside in Latin attire with rain sticks and Cinco de Mayo merchandise.
jokes.punchline: Jerry blames her rain-dance-adjacent behavior for the weather — a completely absurd but perfectly deadpan non-sequitur.
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jerry straight-man deadpan. Six words that connect Elaine's Mayan-cultural-dance-protest to the rain in the most logical-yet-absurd possible way. Highly economical, perfectly delivered in character. A great tiny joke.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, I can't get fired. I've tried everything. I show up late, I do bad work, I insult the clients.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: But they just keep me around. It's like I'm too incompetent to actually be fired for being incompetent.

jokes.text: 'Come celebrate the millennium with "Newmannium."'
jokes.setup: Elaine reads Newman's party invitation
jokes.punchline: Newman has branded his party 'Newmannium' — a portmanteau of his name and millennium
jokes.explanation: 'Newmannium' is a great Seinfeld neologism — it's exactly the kind of self-aggrandizing branding Newman would create. The word is instantly funny just to hear/read, and it perfectly captures Newman's grandiosity. Quotability is high; this is the kind of word that enters usage.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You! I should have known it was you running this place into the ground!

dialogue.speaker: Gladys Mayo
dialogue.line: What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You've been sabotaging me this whole time, haven't you? The fake coupons, the bad inventory...

dialogue.speaker: Gladys Mayo
dialogue.line: Elaine, I think you're confused.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Confused? I'm not confused. I own both stores.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I've been competing against myself this whole time.

jokes.text: Valerie's stepmother corners Jerry in a car and tells him she's 'climbed 13 years to the top of that speed dial' and won't lose her spot to him
jokes.setup: Jerry shows up at Valerie's door
jokes.punchline: He's confronted by the stepmother who has been competing for speed-dial supremacy for 13 years — treating the speed-dial hierarchy as a decades-long power struggle
jokes.explanation: The speed-dial concept reaches its absurdist peak here. The idea that someone has been strategically managing their speed-dial position for 13 years — and is now threatening a man in a car about it — is a perfect escalation of a running joke into something genuinely dark and funny. 'Drive.' is a great button.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: These pretzels are making me thirsty!

jokes.text: George is called in about the meeting — he's already packed his desk and is ready to leave — but Steinbrenner says what happened is 'exactly what this organization needed'
jokes.setup: George expects to be fired for his behavior
jokes.punchline: Steinbrenner inexplicably praises the insults as visionary — 'To look to the future, we've got to tear down the past' — completely inverting George's expectations
jokes.explanation: A great ironic reversal — George can't even succeed at getting fired. Steinbrenner reframes George's sabotage as leadership. The 'I can't do anything wrong' spiral that follows is equally good. Well-structured misdirection with strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Hamilton
dialogue.line: You know, Jerry, when Valerie's away... the mice will play.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Mrs. Hamilton, I don't think that's appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Hamilton
dialogue.line: Oh come on. I've already programmed myself into your speed dial. All you have to do is press one button.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's... that's not how speed dial works.

jokes.text: 'Babe Ruth was nothing more than a fat old man with little-girl legs.'
jokes.setup: Steinbrenner is inspired by George's insults to tear down baseball's past
jokes.punchline: He delivers a vicious physical assessment of Babe Ruth — 'fat old man with little-girl legs' — as organizational philosophy
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The physical specificity — 'little-girl legs' — is both absurd and shockingly vivid. Delivered by Steinbrenner (a character known for grandiosity) as forward-thinking business philosophy, it's a perfect marriage of character and joke. Deeply quotable and darkly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: It's the Newman's Millennium Party!

jokes.text: 'And here's something I just found out recently. He wasn't really a sultan, huh? What do you make of that?'
jokes.setup: Steinbrenner is demolishing Babe Ruth's legacy
jokes.punchline: He reveals he apparently just learned 'The Sultan of Swat' was a nickname, not a literal title — treating this as a major revelation
jokes.explanation: Extraordinary joke. The implication that Steinbrenner, who runs a baseball organization, genuinely believed Babe Ruth was a literal sultan — and has only 'just found out recently' that he wasn't — is a perfect character beat. It makes him simultaneously more absurd and more threatening. The 'What do you make of that?' is a great button. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Baseball Announcer
dialogue.line: And here comes George Costanza, dropping his pants, fully exposing himself to the crowd. Oh my, he's running toward center field now, completely naked, his genitals flapping in the wind. What we're witnessing here is either a cry for help or a man who has completely lost his mind.

jokes.text: Kramer puts on Lou Gehrig's pants from the Hall of Pride and worries the nerve disease might be contagious: 'Better take them off. I'm too important to this team. Big Stein can't be flopping and twitching.'
jokes.setup: Kramer discovers Lou Gehrig's pants and puts them on
jokes.punchline: Steinbrenner worries ALS might be contagious through pants and refers to himself in the third person as 'Big Stein'
jokes.explanation: Dark joke about ALS — the concern about contagion through pants is absurd medical ignorance played straight. 'Big Stein can't be flopping and twitching' is a wonderfully self-important line. The dark subversion of making Lou Gehrig's disease the punchline is Seinfeld at its most transgressive.

dialogue.speaker: Baseball Announcer
dialogue.line: And here comes George Costanza, he's running onto the field, he's dropping his pants!

dialogue.speaker: George Costanza
dialogue.line: Giddy up!

jokes.text: 'That's it. She's off the speed dial completely.'
jokes.setup: Valerie discovers her stepmother got to Jerry
jokes.punchline: The nuclear option is removing someone from the speed dial entirely — Valerie deploys it with maximum drama
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the speed-dial stakes. Removing someone entirely from the speed dial is treated as an act of war — the absurdity of the concept continues to pay off.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: A festivus for the rest of us!

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: That's not even a portmanteau.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Well, what about 'Partyvent'?

jokes.text: 'Cancel? Think again, long shanks.'
jokes.setup: Kramer demands Newman cancel his competing millennium party
jokes.punchline: 'Long shanks' — an archaic insult based on Kramer's tall, lanky frame — delivered by Newman with full dramatic weight
jokes.explanation: Newman calling Kramer 'long shanks' is a wonderfully unexpected insult — archaic, slightly Shakespearean, and completely out of left field. It's surprising enough to be very funny and characterizes Newman's grandiose sense of drama. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: I've booked the top nightclub in the city for New Year's Eve, millennium celebration. I'm the MC.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: The millennium doesn't start until January 1st, 2001.

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: There was no year zero. The first century went from year 1 to year 100. So the second century went from 101 to 200. Which means the 20th century goes from 1901 to 2000. And the 21st century and the new millennium don't begin until January 1st, 2001.

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: But everybody's celebrating on New Year's Eve this year!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I know. They're wrong.

jokes.text: 'I started planning this in 1978. I put a deposit down on that restaurant that overlooks Times Square.'
jokes.setup: Newman insists his party takes precedence
jokes.punchline: He's been planning this millennium party since 1978 — 22 years of preparation for a single party
jokes.explanation: The specificity of '1978' is perfect — it implies Newman has been thinking about this millennium party essentially his entire adult life, which is both very funny and very Newman. Well-integrated character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Hamilton
dialogue.line: I don't understand. Why am I so dry? My skin, my hair... everything is just shriveling up!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, those clothes I gave you had industrial desiccant packets in them.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Hamilton
dialogue.line: Desiccant? You mean those little packets that remove moisture?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I forgot to take them out.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Hamilton
dialogue.line: Kramer! I'm becoming a human raisin!

jokes.text: 'And I booked Christopher Cross.'
jokes.setup: Newman lists the impressive features of his millennium party
jokes.punchline: His marquee entertainment is Christopher Cross — a soft-rock artist whose peak was 1980, making the booking simultaneously ambitious and deeply uncool
jokes.explanation: The Christopher Cross detail is inspired. He's a real artist, which makes it funnier than a made-up name — he's famous enough to be recognizable but exactly the kind of soft-rock act that signals both effort and tragic squareness. Newman has apparently been holding Christopher Cross in reserve for 20 years for this moment. Perfect character integration and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Valerie
dialogue.line: Mrs. Hamilton! Mrs. Hamilton, are you okay?

dialogue.speaker: Valerie
dialogue.line: I'm calling Poison Control!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Hello?

dialogue.speaker: Valerie
dialogue.line: Is this Poison Control?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, this is Jerry.

dialogue.speaker: Valerie
dialogue.line: Well, you're in my phone as Poison Control!

jokes.text: The ice negotiation: 'What kind?' / 'Cubed.' / 'That's good stuff. And you can never have too much ice.'
jokes.setup: Kramer is trying to convince Newman to merge their parties by listing his party assets
jokes.punchline: Newman treats 'cubed ice' as a serious negotiating point and responds with genuine respect ('That's good stuff')
jokes.explanation: A beautifully mundane exchange. The dignity with which both men discuss ice type — as if it were weapons-grade party infrastructure — is perfectly in character. 'You can never have too much ice' as a maxim is both ridiculous and somehow true.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm in the speed dial! I'm a speed dial number! Do you know what this means? I'm essential. I'm poison control!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Finally, I've made it. I'm not just any number in someone's phone - I'm a life-or-death number. If someone swallows drain cleaner, I'm the first person they call.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: This is it. This is the pinnacle. Move over, Mom's house and the pizza place - there's a new number one on the speed dial.

jokes.text: 'No Jerry. Jerry is not invited... For me, the next millennium must be Jerry-free.'
jokes.setup: Kramer and Newman negotiate the terms of co-hosting
jokes.punchline: Newman's one non-negotiable: no Jerry. The millennium is a fresh start and he's using it to excise Jerry from his life
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'Jerry-free' applied to an entire millennium is both absurdly petty and perfectly Newman. His use of the millennium as a vehicle for a personal grudge is wonderful. 'The next millennium must be Jerry-free' is extremely quotable and a great expression of the Newman/Jerry dynamic.

jokes.text: 'Never thought I'd fail at failing.'
jokes.setup: George returns from the Yankees unable to get fired despite his best efforts
jokes.punchline: He articulates his unique existential crisis: he has failed at failing
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best line. 'Never thought I'd fail at failing' is a perfect encapsulation of George Costanza — a man whose entire identity is incompetence, now confronted with competence at incompetence. The paradox is Seinfeldian in the deepest sense: it's about nothing (failing at a job) but it's about everything (George's existential identity). Perfect economy, perfect structure, maximum character integration. This is an anchor-level line.

jokes.text: 'You do everything wrong.' / 'Everything?' / 'Everything.' / 'I have no confidence in you.'
jokes.setup: George is demoralized; Jerry tries to cheer him up by confirming his incompetence as a pep talk
jokes.punchline: Jerry delivers a motivational speech that consists entirely of insults: 'You do everything wrong... I have no confidence in you' — and it works as encouragement
jokes.explanation: A beautiful inversion of the pep talk form. Jerry's encouragement is entirely composed of devastating insults delivered as affirmation — and both characters understand it as such. 'I have no confidence in you' as a motivational statement, accepted and appreciated by George, is a perfect expression of their relationship and George's inverted psychology. Near-anchor quality.

jokes.text: 'I guess I just have to pick myself up, dust myself off, and throw myself right back down again.'
jokes.setup: George is recommitting to his goal of getting fired
jokes.punchline: He inverts the classic inspirational phrase 'pick myself up, dust myself off, and start all over again' — adding 'throw myself right back down again' as the final step
jokes.explanation: A wonderfully crafted subversion of an inspirational cliché. George takes the standard motivational phrase and adds the perfect self-defeating final beat — 'throw myself right back down again.' It's quotable, character-specific, and perfectly constructed. One of the episode's strongest lines.

jokes.text: 'That's the spirit. You suck.'
jokes.setup: George delivers his inverted motivational speech
jokes.punchline: Jerry responds with 'That's the spirit. You suck.' — simultaneously an encouragement and an insult, continuing the inverted pep talk
jokes.explanation: The perfect button on the pep talk sequence. 'That's the spirit. You suck.' is a masterclass in economy — four words that are simultaneously sincere and insulting. It builds perfectly on the inverted-encouragement structure established in #57.

jokes.text: 'Together we can drive Putumayo out of business and make Cinco de Mayo, numero uno de Mayo.'
jokes.setup: Elaine has apparently found an accomplice (the Cinco de Mayo store owner or employee) in her campaign against Putumayo.
jokes.punchline: 'Numero uno de Mayo' — Elaine's rallying cry is a pun on the store name, deploying Spanish with comic earnestness.
jokes.explanation: The rallying cry is cute — 'numero uno de Mayo' is a minor wordplay. Elaine's commitment to a petty retail rivalry is the more substantial joke here; the pun itself is mild. Functional humor.

jokes.text: 'I'm Gladys Mayo.'
jokes.setup: Elaine has been waging a commercial war against Putumayo by shopping at their rival Cinco de Mayo; she confronts the owner
jokes.punchline: The owner of Cinco de Mayo reveals she is also the owner of Putumayo — all of Elaine's sabotage money has gone right back to the same woman
jokes.explanation: A classic Seinfeld structural payoff — the elaborate scheme collapses in the most ironic way possible. 'I'm Gladys Mayo' lands as a perfect comedic bombshell because the setup has been building for the entire episode. All of Elaine's money, time, and effort has been enriching the very person she's been trying to hurt. Excellent earned comedy.

jokes.text: 'This really sticks in my craw.'
jokes.setup: Elaine discovers Gladys Mayo owns both stores
jokes.punchline: Her reaction to the revelation is the muted, formal 'This really sticks in my craw' — underplaying the catastrophe with a slightly archaic phrase
jokes.explanation: The use of 'sticks in my craw' as a response to a major scheme collapse is funny by virtue of its understatement and slightly formal register. Elaine's controlled fury is a good character beat.

jokes.text: 'So attractive, so strong, so comedic.'
jokes.setup: Mrs. Hamilton is flattering Jerry over wine at Valerie's apartment
jokes.punchline: Her list of Jerry's qualities — 'attractive, strong, comedic' — is an odd and escalating set; 'comedic' as a compliment rather than 'funny' is itself slightly off
jokes.explanation: The word 'comedic' as a quality alongside 'attractive' and 'strong' is a small but well-chosen comic detail — it's slightly clinical and off, suggesting Mrs. Hamilton's seduction has a creepy transactional quality.

jokes.text: Mrs. Hamilton's 'deliciously naughty idea' is to put Jerry on her speed dial — treating phone contact as an erotic proposition
jokes.setup: Mrs. Hamilton hints at a naughty idea that Jerry assumes will be something inappropriate
jokes.punchline: Her proposition is to put him on her speed dial — the speed dial has been so thoroughly fetishized that it now functions as a sexual advance
jokes.explanation: The speed-dial concept reaches its apex here: it's been inflated from a relationship barometer to an object of desire to a power struggle to now a sexual proposition. The escalation is perfectly crafted and earns full marks for structure. 'How's number three sound?' as a come-on is hilarious.

jokes.text: 'It's 2000. Newmannium!'
jokes.setup: Jerry and Newman encounter each other
jokes.punchline: Newman announces the new millennium as if personally taking credit for it, branding it 'Newmannium'
jokes.explanation: The 'Newmannium' callback lands well here — Newman's triumphant announcement feels like a genuine victory lap. The self-branding of a year is beautifully absurd.

jokes.text: 'A short, stocky, bald man is streaking across the field. Oh, my God, George. No, check that. He's not streaking. He's wearing a flesh-toned body stocking. Apparently he's a bit bashful, and oddly, no one seems upset.'
jokes.setup: George has gone to a Yankees game in a flesh-toned bodysuit as part of his scheme to get fired
jokes.punchline: The announcer describes him with detail — 'a bit bashful' explaining why he wore a body stocking rather than streaking — and is puzzled that the crowd isn't upset
jokes.explanation: A magnificent set piece. The announcer's description — calling George 'bashful' for wearing a flesh-toned body stocking instead of actually streaking — is perfectly absurd. The detail of George being too modest to actually streak but still running on the field in a body stocking is exactly the kind of specific, character-driven absurdism Seinfeld does best. The crowd reaction of 'oddly, no one seems upset' adds a perfect final note.

jokes.text: 'It's George.' / 'Everyone loves him.' / 'Yeah, I know.'
jokes.setup: The gang watches George on TV running the field in a body stocking
jokes.punchline: George's sabotage attempt has accidentally made him beloved by the crowd — 'everyone loves him' — which is the opposite of what he wanted
jokes.explanation: A perfect structural irony — George's attempt at spectacular failure has backfired into success. 'Everyone loves him' delivered flatly while watching him run a baseball field in a flesh-colored jumpsuit is a great beat. Jerry's resigned 'Yeah, I know' is the perfect button.

jokes.text: Kramer uses a pricing gun to mark everything in Putumayo at 99 cents as the 'sale of the century'
jokes.setup: Kramer and Elaine need a new plan to destroy Putumayo after the pricing gun approach is considered
jokes.punchline: The plan: reprice everything in the store at 99 cents — making them lose money on every sale — framed as 'the sale of the century'
jokes.explanation: A solid Kramer scheme with good internal logic — actually using a pricing gun to undercut a store is funny in its specificity. The plan makes sense in a Kramer way.

jokes.text: 'Still a rip-off.'
jokes.setup: Kramer announces their plan to reprice everything at 99 cents
jokes.punchline: Elaine's immediate, dry response: 'Still a rip-off' — maintaining her deep grievance with the store's pricing even at 99 cents
jokes.explanation: Three-word line that perfectly captures Elaine's character — her grudge is so deep that even 99-cent prices can't satisfy her. Perfect economy and character specificity. A great button on the pricing gun scene.

jokes.text: The three-way speed-dial phone call: Jerry juggling Valerie and Mrs. Hamilton, negotiating speed-dial positions in real time while on hold with both
jokes.setup: Valerie calls Jerry to say she saw him on her stepmother's speed dial; Jerry has to manage both women
jokes.punchline: The conversation becomes an elaborate three-way negotiation about who is on whose speed dial, with Jerry saying 'hang on' repeatedly as he shuttles between calls
jokes.explanation: The speed-dial concept reaches its structural climax: a three-way diplomatic negotiation conducted entirely through speed-dial politics. The escalation is well-paced and the absurdity of the stakes (who is in what speed-dial position) vs. the seriousness of the participants makes for great cringe/escalation comedy.

jokes.text: Jerry/Kramer appears in Putumayo as H.E. Pennypacker, 'a wealthy American industrialist looking to open a silver mine in the mountains of Peru'
jokes.setup: Kramer needs to infiltrate Putumayo for some plan
jokes.punchline: His cover identity is a specific, elaborately titled 'wealthy American industrialist' named H.E. Pennypacker who wants to 'go a little native' and understand 'their condiments, their unmentionables'
jokes.explanation: H.E. Pennypacker is a great Kramer character — the pompous American industrialist persona is absurdly specific. The detail of wanting to understand 'their condiments, their unmentionables' (presumably meaning clothing) is wonderfully confused. The name H.E. Pennypacker itself is great. This is top-tier Kramer absurdism.

jokes.text: 'Machu Picchu. Are these free?'
jokes.setup: H.E. Pennypacker is browsing Putumayo
jokes.punchline: Kramer pauses to marvel at what is presumably a picture or display of Machu Picchu, then asks if the items are free — still fully in character as an industrialist
jokes.explanation: A minor character beat that extends the Pennypacker bit. 'Are these free?' after an exclamation of 'Machu Picchu' is a funny non-sequitur that punctures the pomposity of the character.

jokes.text: Pennypacker is seen trying on women's clothing: 'Some of those are women's clothes.' / 'Oh, not a problem.'
jokes.setup: H.E. Pennypacker is browsing what he assumes are Peruvian goods
jokes.punchline: He's trying on women's clothing and dismisses the distinction entirely with 'Oh, not a problem' — maintaining character perfectly
jokes.explanation: Pennypacker's casual dismissal of gender distinctions in clothing — 'Oh, not a problem' — is funny because it's consistent with his industrialist persona (he's above such concerns) and because Kramer would absolutely try on women's clothes without blinking.

jokes.text: George's public address announcement to Steinbrenner: 'Attention, Steinbrenner and front-office morons. Your triumphs mean nothing. You all stink. You can sit on it and rotate!'
jokes.setup: George is escalating his get-fired campaign with a public address announcement
jokes.punchline: The speech is a perfectly escalating series of insults culminating in 'You can sit on it and rotate!' — a schoolyard taunt delivered with maximum seriousness into a PA system
jokes.explanation: George's PA speech is a great set piece. 'You can sit on it and rotate!' is the perfect childish punchline after the buildup — it's an insult George genuinely believes is devastating. His name and extension at the end ('This is George Costanza. Extension 5170') is an inspired touch of accountability for someone trying to be fired.

jokes.text: 'This is George Costanza. I fear no reprisal. Extension 5170.'
jokes.setup: George has just delivered a blistering PA insult to the entire Yankees organization
jokes.punchline: He signs off with his name, declaration of fearlessness, and his extension — the bureaucratic formality of providing a callback number after an act of mutiny
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's top lines. 'I fear no reprisal. Extension 5170.' is perfection — the macho declaration undercut immediately by the helpful provision of his extension number. It's George in total: an act of bravado immediately undermined by compulsive institutional compliance. Extremely quotable and deeply character-specific. Near-anchor quality.

jokes.text: 'You have screwed me again, Pennypacker.'
jokes.setup: Elaine realizes Kramer's plans have all failed
jokes.punchline: She addresses him as 'Pennypacker' — his alter-ego from the Putumayo infiltration — blaming the fictional character for her real-world problems
jokes.explanation: Elaine's exasperated use of 'Pennypacker' — treating Kramer's cover identity as a separate entity responsible for wrongdoing — is a great character beat. The callback is well-earned and the economy is excellent.

jokes.text: 'That's not gonna do anything.' 'Patience.'
jokes.setup: Elaine points out that removing desiccants from clothes is not an effective act of sabotage.
jokes.punchline: Kramer responds with total serenity: 'Patience.' — implying he's playing a five-year long game.
jokes.explanation: One word, but it's the right word. Kramer's 'Patience' implies he's genuinely committed to a five-year sabotage timeline, which is both absurd and completely in character. The economy score is perfect because the entire comedic concept is carried by a single word.

jokes.text: Pennypacker offers chips to the Putumayo customers while in disguise: 'Ladies, care for some chips?' / 'Well, I don't mind if I do.'
jokes.setup: H.E. Pennypacker is still in the store after his plans have collapsed
jokes.punchline: He offers chips to customers as if hosting a social event — maintaining his industrialist persona through hospitality
jokes.explanation: A nice physical comedy extension of the Pennypacker bit — offering chips in someone else's store is a great absurdist touch. Slightly diminished returns but still lands.

jokes.text: 'Well, I've changed my mind. I think I'm going to build a roller coaster instead.'
jokes.setup: Pennypacker is wrapping up his Putumayo visit
jokes.punchline: He announces he's abandoning the silver mine plan in favor of building a roller coaster — still fully in character as an industrialist whose plans change on a whim
jokes.explanation: A superb Pennypacker button. The industrialist's pivot from silver mining to roller coasters is a perfectly absurd character move — wealthy men do pivot capriciously. The delivery as a goodbye while still in character is committed and funny. Highly quotable as a standalone.

jokes.text: Wilhelm steps forward to take the blame for George's parking-lot rampage, claiming he ordered George to insult everyone
jokes.setup: George is being praised for his insubordination; Wilhelm tries to save him by confessing
jokes.punchline: Wilhelm says he ordered George to insult people because he's 'tired of all your macho head games' — which George immediately steals: 'He's lying, sir. I'm tired of all your macho head games.'
jokes.explanation: A great comedic escalation. Wilhelm tries to sacrifice himself for George; George immediately undercuts him by stealing his exact excuse — 'I'm tired of all your macho head games.' The betrayal is instant and completely Costanza. Well-structured and character-specific.

jokes.text: 'I cannot believe that you, Body-Suit Man, could perpetrate such a disloyal deed. It breaks my heart to say it. Who am I kidding? I love it.'
jokes.setup: Steinbrenner summons George (now nicknamed 'Body-Suit Man') about his parking lot incident.
jokes.punchline: Steinbrenner does a rapid 180 — moving from theatrical outrage to 'Who am I kidding? I love it.' in the same breath, revealing his real feelings instantly and completely undermining the pretense.
jokes.explanation: Steinbrenner's inability to maintain his own indignation is a great character beat. 'Who am I kidding? I love it.' is the comedic engine — the immediate reversal suggests this level of workplace chaos is exactly what Steinbrenner secretly wants. Character integration is high because this is specifically Steinbrenner's brand of erratic authority.

jokes.text: 'Wilhelm, you're fired.'
jokes.setup: Wilhelm has tried to take the fall for George; George has stolen his excuse
jokes.punchline: Steinbrenner fires Wilhelm instead of George — the man trying to get fired watches the wrong person get fired
jokes.explanation: A perfect Seinfeld structural irony — the wrong person gets fired. George has now actively caused someone else to be fired while himself remaining employed. The comedy of George's helpless witness to his own failed plan is great.

jokes.text: 'I'm not going to the game today. I'm going to go outside and scalp some tickets. Owner's box. That's gotta bring in 40 bucks, no problem.'
jokes.setup: Steinbrenner dismisses George and Wilhelm
jokes.punchline: He announces he's going to scalp his own owner's box tickets for $40 — a billionaire scalping his own seats for pocket change
jokes.explanation: An excellent Steinbrenner joke. The image of a team owner scalping his own tickets for $40 captures the character's bizarre combination of power and pettiness. 'That's gotta bring in 40 bucks, no problem' is perfectly calibrated — modest ambition from a man with unlimited resources.

jokes.text: 'I don't know what Elaine is so upset about. Without desiccants, those clothes will be noticeably musty in five years. She never sees the big picture.'
jokes.setup: Kramer reflects on why Elaine rejected his desiccant Plan B
jokes.punchline: He genuinely believes the five-year musti-fication plan is sound strategy and that Elaine lacks vision ('She never sees the big picture')
jokes.explanation: The callback to the desiccant plan, with Kramer fully committed to it as visionary strategy, is wonderfully self-consistent. 'She never sees the big picture' applied to a plan whose results won't be felt for five years is perfectly absurd. The condescension toward Elaine from the man who tried to destroy a store by removing moisture packets is a great beat.

jokes.text: 'Hello, Newman.' / 'Hello, Jerry.'
jokes.setup: Jerry and Newman encounter each other in the hallway
jokes.punchline: Their ritualized greeting — formal, tense, laden with mutual contempt — is its own joke, as always
jokes.explanation: The 'Hello, Newman' exchange is one of Seinfeld's most iconic running gags. Not inventive in this instance but the delivery and audience recognition earn a reliable big laugh. Maximum economy and character integration.

jokes.text: 'Well, maybe so, but come midnight, when she's looking for someone warm and cuddly to kiss, I guess you'll be caught between the moon and New York City.'
jokes.setup: Jerry is trying to lure Elaine away from Newman's party by pointing out she'll be alone at midnight
jokes.punchline: Jerry references the Christopher Cross song 'Arthur's Theme' ('Caught Between the Moon and New York City') — a callback to Newman's booking of Christopher Cross for the party
jokes.explanation: A superb callback. Jerry references the Christopher Cross song lyric from 'Arthur's Theme' to taunt Newman about his party — a perfect marriage of the Christopher Cross detail and Jerry's wit. The callback is earned and the joke works on multiple levels: it's a dig at Newman, a pop culture reference, and a demonstration of Jerry's ability to weaponize any information. One of the episode's best crafted moments.

jokes.text: Newman immediately folds and invites Jerry back to the party after Jerry's Christopher Cross jab
jokes.setup: Newman has held firm that the Newmannium must be Jerry-free
jokes.punchline: One well-placed jab and Newman completely abandons his principled stance — 'All right. Come back to my party, please.'
jokes.explanation: Newman's capitulation is fast and funny — his millennium commitment crumbles the moment he's teased. Good character beat showing Newman's bluster is all surface.

jokes.text: 'You don't want to do your act or anything, do you?' — Newman, while agreeing to let Jerry come to the Newmannium.
jokes.setup: Newman reluctantly agrees Jerry can come to the party in exchange for Elaine attending.
jokes.punchline: His only specific concern about Jerry is whether Jerry will do 'his act' — suggesting the worst thing about Jerry isn't his personality but his stand-up comedy.
jokes.explanation: A subtle meta-joke — Newman's specific concern is that Jerry might do stand-up comedy, implying his comedy is the most intolerable thing about him. Given that this is a show where Jerry is a stand-up comedian, this is a slight self-referential dig. Character-specific and neatly economical.

jokes.text: The toast: 'To the Newmannium.' / 'To the Kramennium.'
jokes.setup: Newman and Kramer are toasting their merged party
jokes.punchline: Kramer counter-brands with 'Kramennium' — refusing to cede the neologism entirely
jokes.explanation: A great callback button. Kramer's assertion of 'Kramennium' as an equal and simultaneous branding is perfectly in character — he won't let Newman monopolize the linguistic territory. The toast as a competition is very funny.

jokes.text: Jerry reveals the millennium doesn't start until 2001 because there was no year zero — making Newman's party 'one year late and thus quite lame'
jokes.setup: The party plans are settled; everyone is happy with the Newmannium arrangement
jokes.punchline: Jerry drops the historical fact that there was no year zero, meaning the millennium technically begins in 2001 — making the entire episode's premise and Newman's life's work 'quite lame'
jokes.explanation: The episode's structural masterstroke. The entire episode — all of Newman's planning since 1978, the deposit on the Times Square restaurant, the Christopher Cross booking, the Newmannium — is annihilated by a single historical fact delivered by Jerry with perfect smugness. The pause after 'thus quite lame' while Newman processes is devastating. This is a top-tier episode-ending joke that recontextualizes everything. Jerry's 'thus quite lame' is the perfect, aristocratic phrasing for maximum damage.

jokes.text: Mrs. Hamilton is violently ill from the desiccants; Valerie hits Poison Control and gets Jerry
jokes.setup: The speed-dial comedy and the desiccant subplot collide in the final scene
jokes.punchline: Mrs. Hamilton has apparently ingested the desiccants; Valerie tries Poison Control but gets Jerry because he was hidden 'in one of the emergency buttons'
jokes.explanation: A masterful convergence ending that ties together three separate running jokes — the speed-dial politics, the desiccant sabotage, and Jerry's placement in the emergency buttons. Mrs. Hamilton is ill from desiccants (Kramer's plan has circled back to bite everyone), and Valerie's Poison Control call gets Jerry because he was hidden in the emergency slot during the speed-dial negotiation. The structural payoff is exceptional Seinfeld plotting.

jokes.text: 'I don't feel well at all. I feel all dried-out inside.' — Mrs. Hamilton, having consumed desiccants.
jokes.setup: The episode needs a final coda; Mrs. Hamilton is violently ill.
jokes.punchline: She feels 'all dried-out inside' — the symptoms consistent with having ingested the desiccants Kramer removed from the Putumayo clothes, which Elaine presumably served as chips.
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly engineered multi-storyline convergence. Mrs. Hamilton has ingested the desiccants from Kramer's sabotage plan (served at some point as 'chips'), and her symptom — 'dried-out inside' — is both the physical consequence and the perfect descriptive payoff of what desiccants do. This is textbook Seinfeld plotting: disparate subplots collide with a victim who has no idea what's happened to her. The 'dried-out inside' line is precisely right. Earns 8.5.

jokes.text: 'Wow, Poison Control. That's even higher than number one.'
jokes.setup: Valerie tells Jerry she hit the button for Poison Control and got him
jokes.punchline: Jerry's response is not concern for Mrs. Hamilton but delight at his new speed-dial status — Poison Control outranks number one
jokes.explanation: The episode's perfect capper. Jerry's response to learning that someone is having a medical emergency caused by calling him instead of Poison Control is pure delight at his speed-dial promotion. 'Poison Control. That's even higher than number one' is the ultimate expression of Jerry's self-absorption and the episode's central joke: that these people have treated a phone contact list as the most important thing in their lives. One of the best closing lines in the episode.

tmdb_overview: The Mets express interest in George; an inattentive saleswoman irks Elaine; Kramer prepares for his millennium party; Jerry jockeys for position on his girlfriend's speed dial.
insight: Y2K anxiety spawns 46 jokes in 23 minutes—character-driven absurdism at peak density.
